reasoning: The USMCA review mechanism (Article 34.7) was specifically designed by the Trump administration to create leverage by forcing renegotiations under the threat of a 16-year expiration clock. A prompt extension explicitly resets this clock, removing the US's primary leverage. As of April 2026, formal talks with Canada have not yet begun, and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer has stated publicly that the US will likely not resolve issues by the July 1 deadline. Extending the agreement promptly would require the US to abandon its structural negotiating advantage before securing its extensive demands (e.g., changes to auto rules of origin, actions against non-market economies). The explicit design of the agreement expects and encourages the triggering of Paragraph 4 (annual reviews) to maintain pressure on Mexico and Canada. Therefore, the joint review will almost certainly end without a prompt 16-year extension, triggering the annual review process and resolving the question as No.
